title: On the Matter of the Murder of Ptolemaeus
summary: The body of murder victim Ptolemaeus is said to have been found in a well near Poan and Moeris Ibion and to have been sent to the necropolis. The bottom part of the papyrus contains an account of wheat.  The relationship between the two parts is unclear.

title: Demotic Marriage Contract
summary: Marriage contract dated to the reign of Ptolemy V (but after 199) and containing the so-called "document concerning money," which serves as a security for the "document of maintenance" referred to in line 5.  Party A (the man) is probably some kind of artisan.  Party B (the woman) is lost.

title: A List of Personal Names
summary: A list of Egyptian names and patronymics.

title: Document on Shoe-Shaped Papyrus
summary: Copy of a letter written by Sarapion, a member of the First Friends and a dioiketes (or hypodioiketes), concerning a death and, it seems, subsequent division of a kleros.  The document also mentions a scribe of the Machimoi whose name no longer survives.

title: An Account of Money and a List of Names
summary: This is a large papyrus containing an account of money on the front side.  The back is dominated by a list with days of the week followed by personal names, a few of which are repeated numerous times.

title: Greek Letter
summary: Letter addressed to Posidonius requesting him to come to the city with his accounts (logoi).

title: Letter from Leonides
summary: Left side of a letter from Leonides.  Mentions a man named Democrates.

title: A Ptolemaic Petition to the Archidikastes
summary: A woman named Senyris submits a petition to the chief judge (archidikastes), Demophon, against a man whose name is lost but who is described as the son of Pancrates and father of Senyris?s two children.  She charges the man with breaking his contract of maintenance (a kind of loose marriage contract) and running off with another woman.  The parties to the petition (both Senyris and the man) are Egyptian and the archidikastes, who would have resided at Alexandria, is Greek, illustrating the reach and importance of the Greek judiciary system among the native Egyptian population.  Not many papyri from the Ptolemaic period survive that attest the office of the archidikastes.

title: Greek Document Mentioning Krokodilon polis
summary: Fragment of a document that mentions Krokodilon polis, capital of the Arsinoite nome, and refers to a Mikythos and Sokomenis.  The former name is relatively uncommon in papyri.

title: Part of Two Columns from an Official Register
summary: Remains of an official register, perhaps of property.  Mention is made of someone who died childless.

title: Greek Papyrus
summary: A much-worn fragment mentioning on one side an individual named Panontos and on the other an attendant of the public archive (hyperetes bibliothekes).
